Real Analysis

Quiz Problems and Combination Theorems for Uniform Continuity

Quiz Problems and Combination Theorems for Uniform Continuity

This lecture consolidates limit and continuity techniques through worked problems, then builds up a toolkit of ways to combine uniformly continuous functions — reciprocals, products, and piecewise gluing — culminating in a full analysis of x\sqrt{x}x​ on [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞).

Quiz Problem 1: A Polynomial Limit via Factoring

This is the polynomial analogue of the reciprocal and square-root ε\varepsilonε–δ\deltaδ proofs from Lectures 13 and 16: factor out (x−c)(x-c)(x−c), then bound the remaining factor on a restricted neighborhood.

lim⁡x→2(x2+3x)\lim_{x\to 2} (x^2+3x) limx→2​(x2+3x) — Correction and Method

We illustrate the method using the factoring identity x3−8=(x−2)(x2+2x+4)x^3 - 8 = (x-2)(x^2+2x+4)x3−8=(x−2)(x2+2x+4), which is the general technique for a cubic limit at c=2c=2c=2. Starting from the target inequality,

∣x3+3x−14∣<ϵ.|x^3+3x-14| < \epsilon.∣x3+3x−14∣<ϵ.

Wherever the expression factors as (x−c)⋅(remaining polynomial)(x-c)\cdot(\text{remaining polynomial})(x−c)⋅(remaining polynomial), e.g.

∣(x−2)(x2+2x+7)∣<ϵ⟺∣x2+2x+7∣⋅∣x−2∣<ϵ,|(x-2)(x^2+2x+7)| < \epsilon \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad |x^2+2x+7|\cdot|x-2| < \epsilon,∣(x−2)(x2+2x+7)∣<ϵ⟺∣x2+2x+7∣⋅∣x−2∣<ϵ,

the strategy is to bound the polynomial factor ∣x2+2x+7∣|x^2+2x+7|∣x2+2x+7∣ by a constant MMM on a restricted neighborhood of x=2x=2x=2, then solve for δ\deltaδ.

Restrict xxx first. Impose ∣x−2∣<1|x-2| < 1∣x−2∣<1, so 1<x<31 < x < 31<x<3. On this range, the polynomial factor is bounded: ∣x2+2x+7∣≤M|x^2+2x+7| \le M∣x2+2x+7∣≤M for an explicit constant MMM (e.g. M=22M = 22M=22, obtained by plugging in the endpoint x=3x=3x=3).

Combine the two restrictions. Choose

δ:=min⁡{1, ϵM}.\delta := \min\left\{1,\ \frac{\epsilon}{M}\right\}.δ:=min{1, Mϵ​}.

Then for ∣x−2∣<δ|x-2| < \delta∣x−2∣<δ,

∣x2+2x+7∣⋅∣x−2∣≤M∣x−2∣<M⋅ϵM=ϵ.|x^2+2x+7|\cdot|x-2| \le M|x-2| < M\cdot\frac{\epsilon}{M} = \epsilon.∣x2+2x+7∣⋅∣x−2∣≤M∣x−2∣<M⋅Mϵ​=ϵ.
The general template

This is the same two-step recipe used for x\sqrt{x}x​ in Lecture 16: first impose a preliminary restriction (like ∣x−2∣<1|x-2|<1∣x−2∣<1) to bound the “extra” factor by a constant MMM, then solve M∣x−c∣<ϵM|x-c|<\epsilonM∣x−c∣<ϵ for the fine-grained bound, and finally take δ\deltaδ to be the minimum of the two restrictions.

Quiz Problem 2: Convergence via a Contraction Hypothesis on fff

This example reduces to the Contractive Sequence Theorem of Lecture 12: rather than being handed a contractive bound on ∣xn+1−xn∣|x_{n+1}-x_n|∣xn+1​−xn​∣ directly, we derive one from a Lipschitz-type hypothesis on the generating function fff.

Setup and First Step

Suppose f:R→Rf: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}f:R→R satisfies ∣f(x)−f(y)∣≤13∣x−y∣|f(x)-f(y)| \le \tfrac13|x-y|∣f(x)−f(y)∣≤31​∣x−y∣ for all x,y∈Rx,y \in \mathbb{R}x,y∈R (a Lipschitz condition with constant C=1/3C = 1/3C=1/3, in the language of Lecture 18), and define xn+1=f(xn)x_{n+1} = f(x_n)xn+1​=f(xn​). Then

∣xn+2−xn+1∣=∣f(xn+1)−f(xn)∣≤C∣xn+1−xn∣,|x_{n+2}-x_{n+1}| = |f(x_{n+1})-f(x_n)| \le C|x_{n+1}-x_n|,∣xn+2​−xn+1​∣=∣f(xn+1​)−f(xn​)∣≤C∣xn+1​−xn​∣,

directly from the hypothesis on fff, with C=1/3∈(0,1)C = 1/3 \in (0,1)C=1/3∈(0,1).

Completing the argument

This is exactly the contractive condition from Lecture 12, so (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is contractive and hence Cauchy, hence convergent by the Cauchy Convergence Criterion. Note the broader pattern: any function satisfying a Lipschitz bound with constant strictly less than 111 (a contraction mapping) automatically generates a convergent iteration sequence — this is the one-dimensional case of the Banach Fixed Point Theorem.

Quiz Problem 3: A Function Positive at a Point Stays Positive Nearby

This applies the ε\varepsilonε–δ\deltaδ definition of continuity (Lecture 13) with a cleverly chosen ε\varepsilonε tied to the value f(x0)f(x_0)f(x0​) itself, rather than an arbitrary target.

Statement and Proof

Given: fff is continuous at x0x_0x0​ in the ε\varepsilonε–δ\deltaδ sense. Suppose additionally f:(a,b)→Rf: (a,b) \to \mathbb{R}f:(a,b)→R is continuous at x0∈(a,b)x_0 \in (a,b)x0​∈(a,b) with f(x0)>0f(x_0) > 0f(x0​)>0. We show there is an interval I∋x0I \ni x_0I∋x0​, I⊆(a,b)I \subseteq (a,b)I⊆(a,b), on which fff stays positive.

Take ϵ=f(x0)2>0\epsilon = \dfrac{f(x_0)}{2} > 0ϵ=2f(x0​)​>0 (valid since f(x0)>0f(x_0)>0f(x0​)>0). By continuity at x0x_0x0​, there exists δ(ϵ)>0\delta(\epsilon) > 0δ(ϵ)>0 such that for all x∈(a,b)x \in (a,b)x∈(a,b) with ∣x−x0∣<δ(ϵ)|x-x_0| < \delta(\epsilon)∣x−x0​∣<δ(ϵ),

∣f(x)−f(x0)∣<f(x0)2⟹f(x0)2<f(x)<3f(x0)2.|f(x)-f(x_0)| < \frac{f(x_0)}{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{f(x_0)}{2} < f(x) < \frac{3f(x_0)}{2}.∣f(x)−f(x0​)∣<2f(x0​)​⟹2f(x0​)​<f(x)<23f(x0​)​.

In particular f(x)>f(x0)/2>0f(x) > f(x_0)/2 > 0f(x)>f(x0​)/2>0 on this neighborhood. Taking

I={x:∣x−x0∣<δ(ϵ)}∩(a,b)I = \{x : |x-x_0| < \delta(\epsilon)\} \cap (a,b)I={x:∣x−x0​∣<δ(ϵ)}∩(a,b)

gives the desired interval: I⊆(a,b)I \subseteq (a,b)I⊆(a,b), x0∈Ix_0 \in Ix0​∈I, and f(x)>0f(x) > 0f(x)>0 for all x∈Ix \in Ix∈I.

Why halving f(x0)f(x_0)f(x0​) is the right choice

Choosing ϵ=f(x0)/2\epsilon = f(x_0)/2ϵ=f(x0​)/2 guarantees the resulting interval (f(x0)/2, 3f(x0)/2)\bigl(f(x_0)/2,\ 3f(x_0)/2\bigr)(f(x0​)/2, 3f(x0​)/2) for f(x)f(x)f(x) sits entirely above 000 — any smaller multiple of f(x0)f(x_0)f(x0​) would work too, but half is the standard choice since it keeps the algebra clean. This is a common trick whenever continuity needs to preserve a strict sign, not just approximate a value.

Quiz Problem 4: A Ratio Diverging to −∞-\infty−∞ with Bounded Numerator

This is the “reciprocal tends to zero” corollary from Lecture 13, adapted to a ratio of two sequences rather than a single properly divergent sequence — we give two proofs, one via the Squeeze Theorem and one via a direct ε\varepsilonε–NNN argument.

Statement

Suppose lim⁡n→∞xnyn=−∞\lim_{n\to\infty} \dfrac{x_n}{y_n} = -\inftylimn→∞​yn​xn​​=−∞ and (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded. Show lim⁡n→∞yn=0\lim_{n\to\infty} y_n = 0limn→∞​yn​=0.

Proof via the Squeeze Theorem

Since xn/yn→−∞x_n/y_n \to -\inftyxn​/yn​→−∞, its reciprocal tends to 000 (Reciprocal Tends to Zero, Lecture 13):

ynxn=1xn/yn→0.\frac{y_n}{x_n} = \frac{1}{x_n/y_n} \to 0.xn​yn​​=xn​/yn​1​→0.

Since (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded, there exists M>0M>0M>0 with ∣xn∣<M|x_n| < M∣xn​∣<M for all nnn. Writing yn=ynxn⋅xny_n = \dfrac{y_n}{x_n}\cdot x_nyn​=xn​yn​​⋅xn​,

∣yn∣=∣ynxn∣⋅∣xn∣≤M∣ynxn∣,|y_n| = \left|\frac{y_n}{x_n}\right|\cdot|x_n| \le M\left|\frac{y_n}{x_n}\right|,∣yn​∣=​xn​yn​​​⋅∣xn​∣≤M​xn​yn​​​,

so

−M∣ynxn∣≤yn≤M∣ynxn∣.-M\left|\frac{y_n}{x_n}\right| \le y_n \le M\left|\frac{y_n}{x_n}\right|.−M​xn​yn​​​≤yn​≤M​xn​yn​​​.

Both bounds tend to 000 (since yn/xn→0y_n/x_n \to 0yn​/xn​→0 and MMM is fixed), so by the Squeeze Theorem, yn→0y_n \to 0yn​→0.

Proof via ε\varepsilonε–NNN Directly

Since (xn)(x_n)(xn​) is bounded, fix M>0M > 0M>0 with ∣xn∣<M|x_n| < M∣xn​∣<M for all nnn. Given ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0, set α=−M/ϵ<0\alpha = -M/\epsilon < 0α=−M/ϵ<0. Since xn/yn→−∞x_n/y_n \to -\inftyxn​/yn​→−∞, there exists N(α)N(\alpha)N(α) such that for all n≥N(α)n \ge N(\alpha)n≥N(α),

xnyn<α=−Mϵ⟹∣xnyn∣>Mϵ⟹∣xn∣∣yn∣>Mϵ.\frac{x_n}{y_n} < \alpha = -\frac{M}{\epsilon} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \left|\frac{x_n}{y_n}\right| > \frac{M}{\epsilon} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{|x_n|}{|y_n|} > \frac{M}{\epsilon}.yn​xn​​<α=−ϵM​⟹​yn​xn​​​>ϵM​⟹∣yn​∣∣xn​∣​>ϵM​.

Since ∣xn∣<M|x_n| < M∣xn​∣<M, this gives M∣yn∣>∣xn∣∣yn∣>Mϵ\dfrac{M}{|y_n|} > \dfrac{|x_n|}{|y_n|} > \dfrac{M}{\epsilon}∣yn​∣M​>∣yn​∣∣xn​∣​>ϵM​, so ∣yn∣<ϵ|y_n| < \epsilon∣yn​∣<ϵ. Hence yn→0y_n \to 0yn​→0.

Two routes to the same place

The Squeeze Theorem proof is shorter because it reuses the Reciprocal Tends to Zero corollary as a black box; the direct ε\varepsilonε–NNN proof re-derives that fact from scratch in the course of the argument. Both are valid — the first favors reusing prior results, the second is self-contained.

Combination Theorems for Uniform Continuity

We now return to uniform continuity (Lecture 18) and develop three ways of building new uniformly continuous functions from old ones: taking reciprocals, taking products, and gluing pieces together across a shared boundary point.

Reciprocals of Uniformly Continuous Functions

The reciprocal map t↦1/tt \mapsto 1/tt↦1/t is itself only Lipschitz (hence uniformly continuous) away from 000 — precisely the obstruction seen with f(x)=1/xf(x)=1/xf(x)=1/x near 000 in Lecture 18. The theorem below shows that composing with a uniformly continuous fff bounded away from 000 avoids this obstruction entirely.

Reciprocal of a Uniformly Continuous Function

Let fff be uniformly continuous on A⊆RA \subseteq \mathbb{R}A⊆R, and suppose there exists k>0k > 0k>0 such that ∣f(x)∣≥k|f(x)| \ge k∣f(x)∣≥k for all x∈Ax \in Ax∈A. Then g(x):=1f(x)g(x) := \dfrac{1}{f(x)}g(x):=f(x)1​ is uniformly continuous on AAA.

The idea

Taking reciprocals is only safe when the function stays away from 000 — this is exactly what ∣f(x)∣≥k|f(x)| \ge k∣f(x)∣≥k guarantees. The identity

∣1f(x)−1f(y)∣=∣f(x)−f(y)∣∣f(x)f(y)∣\left|\frac{1}{f(x)} - \frac{1}{f(y)}\right| = \frac{|f(x)-f(y)|}{|f(x)f(y)|}​f(x)1​−f(y)1​​=∣f(x)f(y)∣∣f(x)−f(y)∣​

converts control of ∣f(x)−f(y)∣|f(x)-f(y)|∣f(x)−f(y)∣ (from uniform continuity of fff) into control of the reciprocal difference, once the denominator is bounded below by k2k^2k2.

Proof

Let ε>0\varepsilon > 0ε>0. Since fff is uniformly continuous on AAA, apply the definition with target value k2ε>0k^2\varepsilon > 0k2ε>0: there exists δ>0\delta > 0δ>0 such that for all x,y∈Ax,y \in Ax,y∈A,

∣x−y∣<δ  ⟹  ∣f(x)−f(y)∣<k2ε.|x-y| < \delta \implies |f(x)-f(y)| < k^2\varepsilon.∣x−y∣<δ⟹∣f(x)−f(y)∣<k2ε.

Then for such x,yx,yx,y,

∣1f(x)−1f(y)∣=∣f(x)−f(y)∣∣f(x)∣∣f(y)∣≤∣f(x)−f(y)∣k2<k2εk2=ε,\left|\frac{1}{f(x)}-\frac{1}{f(y)}\right| = \frac{|f(x)-f(y)|}{|f(x)||f(y)|} \le \frac{|f(x)-f(y)|}{k^2} < \frac{k^2\varepsilon}{k^2} = \varepsilon,​f(x)1​−f(y)1​​=∣f(x)∣∣f(y)∣∣f(x)−f(y)∣​≤k2∣f(x)−f(y)∣​<k2k2ε​=ε,

using ∣f(x)∣,∣f(y)∣≥k|f(x)|,|f(y)| \ge k∣f(x)∣,∣f(y)∣≥k to bound the denominator below by k2k^2k2. Hence g=1/fg = 1/fg=1/f is uniformly continuous on AAA.

The general pattern

This is a composition argument: uniform continuity of fff supplies control on ∣f(x)−f(y)∣|f(x)-f(y)|∣f(x)−f(y)∣ in terms of ∣x−y∣|x-y|∣x−y∣; the lower bound ∣f(x)∣≥k|f(x)| \ge k∣f(x)∣≥k ensures f(A)f(A)f(A) stays inside the region (−∞,−k]∪[k,∞)(-\infty,-k]\cup[k,\infty)(−∞,−k]∪[k,∞), where t↦1/tt\mapsto1/tt↦1/t is itself Lipschitz (with constant 1/k21/k^21/k2, by the same computation as Example 2 of Lecture 18). Combining a uniformly continuous “inner” map with a Lipschitz “outer” map on the relevant range gives a uniformly continuous composition.

Products of Uniformly Continuous Functions

Unlike the reciprocal, a product of two uniformly continuous functions is not automatically uniformly continuous — boundedness of both factors is needed as well, to control the cross terms that appear when expanding the difference of a product.

Product of Uniformly Continuous, Bounded Functions

If f,gf,gf,g are uniformly continuous and bounded on a common domain, then fgfgfg is uniformly continuous.

The idea

Uniform continuity controls ∣f(x)−f(y)∣|f(x)-f(y)|∣f(x)−f(y)∣ and ∣g(x)−g(y)∣|g(x)-g(y)|∣g(x)−g(y)∣; boundedness controls the sizes of ∣f(x)∣|f(x)|∣f(x)∣ and ∣g(x)∣|g(x)|∣g(x)∣ that multiply those differences after expanding the product. Both ingredients are needed together.

Proof

Suppose ∣f(x)∣≤M|f(x)| \le M∣f(x)∣≤M and ∣g(x)∣≤M|g(x)| \le M∣g(x)∣≤M for all xxx in the domain. Let ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0. By uniform continuity of fff and ggg, choose δf,δg>0\delta_f, \delta_g > 0δf​,δg​>0 such that

∣x−y∣<δf  ⟹  ∣f(x)−f(y)∣<ϵ2M,∣x−y∣<δg  ⟹  ∣g(x)−g(y)∣<ϵ2M.|x-y| < \delta_f \implies |f(x)-f(y)| < \frac{\epsilon}{2M}, \qquad |x-y| < \delta_g \implies |g(x)-g(y)| < \frac{\epsilon}{2M}.∣x−y∣<δf​⟹∣f(x)−f(y)∣<2Mϵ​,∣x−y∣<δg​⟹∣g(x)−g(y)∣<2Mϵ​.

Let δ=min⁡{δf,δg}\delta = \min\{\delta_f,\delta_g\}δ=min{δf​,δg​}. For ∣x−y∣<δ|x-y| < \delta∣x−y∣<δ, insert and subtract a cross term — the same technique used in the Product Limit Law for sequences (Lecture 8):

∣f(x)g(x)−f(y)g(y)∣=∣g(x)(f(x)−f(y))+f(y)(g(x)−g(y))∣≤∣g(x)∣ ∣f(x)−f(y)∣+∣f(y)∣ ∣g(x)−g(y)∣≤M⋅ϵ2M+M⋅ϵ2M=ϵ.\begin{aligned} |f(x)g(x)-f(y)g(y)| &= |g(x)(f(x)-f(y)) + f(y)(g(x)-g(y))| \\ &\le |g(x)|\,|f(x)-f(y)| + |f(y)|\,|g(x)-g(y)| \\ &\le M\cdot\frac{\epsilon}{2M} + M\cdot\frac{\epsilon}{2M} = \epsilon. \end{aligned}∣f(x)g(x)−f(y)g(y)∣​=∣g(x)(f(x)−f(y))+f(y)(g(x)−g(y))∣≤∣g(x)∣∣f(x)−f(y)∣+∣f(y)∣∣g(x)−g(y)∣≤M⋅2Mϵ​+M⋅2Mϵ​=ϵ.​

Hence fgfgfg is uniformly continuous.

Recognizing the recurring three-step pattern

This proof follows the same three steps every time a product needs to be controlled: (1) rewrite the difference using the cross-term identity f(x)g(x)−f(y)g(y)=g(x)(f(x)−f(y))+f(y)(g(x)−g(y))f(x)g(x)-f(y)g(y) = g(x)(f(x)-f(y)) + f(y)(g(x)-g(y))f(x)g(x)−f(y)g(y)=g(x)(f(x)−f(y))+f(y)(g(x)−g(y)), (2) bound the coefficients g(x),f(y)g(x), f(y)g(x),f(y) using boundedness, and (3) make the difference terms small using uniform continuity. This is exactly the structure of the Product Limit Law proof for sequences in Lecture 8 — uniform continuity is simply the function-level analogue of sequential convergence throughout.

Gluing: Uniform Continuity of x\sqrt{x}x​ on [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞)

Neither the Lipschitz criterion (Lecture 18) nor the Uniform Continuity Theorem alone handles x\sqrt{x}x​ on all of [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞): x\sqrt{x}x​ is not Lipschitz near 000 (its “derivative” 12x\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt x}2x​1​ is unbounded there), and [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞) is unbounded, so the Uniform Continuity Theorem of Lecture 18 doesn’t directly apply either. The fix is to split the domain into a compact piece (handled by the Uniform Continuity Theorem) and a Lipschitz piece, then glue the two estimates together at the shared boundary point.

Uniform Continuity of x\sqrt{x}x​ on [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞)

f(x)=xf(x) = \sqrt{x}f(x)=x​ is uniformly continuous on [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞).

Proof

Step 1: Lipschitz on [a,∞)[a,\infty)[a,∞) for fixed a>0a>0a>0. Using the Conjugate Trick (Lecture 6),

∣x−y∣=∣x−yx+y∣=∣x−y∣x+y.|\sqrt{x}-\sqrt{y}| = \left|\frac{x-y}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{y}}\right| = \frac{|x-y|}{\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{y}}.∣x​−y​∣=​x​+y​x−y​​=x​+y​∣x−y∣​.

If x,y∈[a,∞)x,y \in [a,\infty)x,y∈[a,∞), then x+y≥2a\sqrt{x}+\sqrt{y} \ge 2\sqrt{a}x​+y​≥2a​, so

∣x−y∣≤12a∣x−y∣.|\sqrt{x}-\sqrt{y}| \le \frac{1}{2\sqrt{a}}|x-y|.∣x​−y​∣≤2a​1​∣x−y∣.

Thus x\sqrt{x}x​ is Lipschitz on [a,∞)[a,\infty)[a,∞) with constant 12a\tfrac{1}{2\sqrt a}2a​1​, hence uniformly continuous there (Lecture 18).

Step 2: Uniformly continuous on [0,a][0,a][0,a]. Since [0,a][0,a][0,a] is closed and bounded, and x\sqrt{x}x​ is continuous on it, the Uniform Continuity Theorem (Lecture 18) directly applies: x\sqrt{x}x​ is uniformly continuous on [0,a][0,a][0,a].

Step 3: Glue the two pieces. It remains to handle x∈[0,a]x \in [0,a]x∈[0,a], y∈[a,∞)y \in [a,\infty)y∈[a,∞) together. Insert the shared point aaa and apply the triangle inequality:

∣f(x)−f(y)∣=∣f(x)−f(a)+f(a)−f(y)∣≤∣f(x)−f(a)∣+∣f(a)−f(y)∣.|f(x)-f(y)| = |f(x)-f(a) + f(a)-f(y)| \le |f(x)-f(a)| + |f(a)-f(y)|.∣f(x)−f(y)∣=∣f(x)−f(a)+f(a)−f(y)∣≤∣f(x)−f(a)∣+∣f(a)−f(y)∣.

Given ϵ>0\epsilon > 0ϵ>0: by uniform continuity on [0,a][0,a][0,a], there is δ1>0\delta_1 > 0δ1​>0 making ∣f(x)−f(a)∣<ϵ/2|f(x)-f(a)| < \epsilon/2∣f(x)−f(a)∣<ϵ/2 whenever ∣x−a∣<δ1|x-a| < \delta_1∣x−a∣<δ1​; by uniform continuity (Lipschitz) on [a,∞)[a,\infty)[a,∞), there is δ2>0\delta_2 > 0δ2​>0 making ∣f(a)−f(y)∣<ϵ/2|f(a)-f(y)| < \epsilon/2∣f(a)−f(y)∣<ϵ/2 whenever ∣y−a∣<δ2|y-a| < \delta_2∣y−a∣<δ2​. Taking δ=min⁡{δ1,δ2}\delta = \min\{\delta_1,\delta_2\}δ=min{δ1​,δ2​}, if ∣x−y∣<δ|x-y| < \delta∣x−y∣<δ with x∈[0,a]x\in[0,a]x∈[0,a], y∈[a,∞)y\in[a,\infty)y∈[a,∞), then both ∣x−a∣<δ|x-a|<\delta∣x−a∣<δ and ∣y−a∣<δ|y-a|<\delta∣y−a∣<δ (since x,a,yx,a,yx,a,y are ordered), so

∣f(x)−f(y)∣<ϵ2+ϵ2=ϵ.|f(x)-f(y)| < \frac{\epsilon}{2}+\frac{\epsilon}{2} = \epsilon.∣f(x)−f(y)∣<2ϵ​+2ϵ​=ϵ.

Combining all three cases (x,yx,yx,y both in [0,a][0,a][0,a]; both in [a,∞)[a,\infty)[a,∞); or split across the two), f(x)=xf(x)=\sqrt{x}f(x)=x​ is uniformly continuous on all of [0,∞)[0,\infty)[0,∞).

The gluing technique, in general

This three-step pattern — establish uniform continuity separately on a compact piece and an unbounded-but-Lipschitz piece, then bound the cross-boundary case via the triangle inequality through the shared point — is a general method for extending uniform continuity from pieces of a domain to their union, whenever the pieces meet at a single point.

Is f(x)=11+x2f(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}f(x)=1+x21​ Uniformly Continuous on R\mathbb{R}R?

This final example uses the Mean Value Theorem connection from Lecture 18: bound ∣f′(x)∣|f'(x)|∣f′(x)∣ uniformly, and Lipschitz continuity (hence uniform continuity) on all of R\mathbb{R}R follows immediately.

Bounding the Derivative

By the Quotient Rule, f′(x)=−2x(1+x2)2f'(x) = \dfrac{-2x}{(1+x^2)^2}f′(x)=(1+x2)2−2x​. We claim ∣f′(x)∣≤1|f'(x)| \le 1∣f′(x)∣≤1 for all xxx, i.e.

2∣x∣(1+x2)2≤1.\frac{2|x|}{(1+x^2)^2} \le 1.(1+x2)22∣x∣​≤1.

Note that (∣x∣−1)2≥0(|x|-1)^2 \ge 0(∣x∣−1)2≥0 expands to

∣x∣2−2∣x∣+1≥0⟺1+x2≥2∣x∣.|x|^2 - 2|x| + 1 \ge 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad 1+x^2 \ge 2|x|.∣x∣2−2∣x∣+1≥0⟺1+x2≥2∣x∣.

Dividing both sides by (1+x2)2>0(1+x^2)^2 > 0(1+x2)2>0 (and using 1+x2≥2∣x∣1+x^2 \ge 2|x|1+x2≥2∣x∣, together with 1+x2≥11+x^2\ge 11+x2≥1),

2∣x∣(1+x2)2≤1+x2(1+x2)2=11+x2≤1.\frac{2|x|}{(1+x^2)^2} \le \frac{1+x^2}{(1+x^2)^2} = \frac{1}{1+x^2} \le 1.(1+x2)22∣x∣​≤(1+x2)21+x2​=1+x21​≤1.

Hence ∣f′(x)∣≤1|f'(x)| \le 1∣f′(x)∣≤1 for all x∈Rx \in \mathbb{R}x∈R.

Conclusion via the MVT connection

By the Bounded Derivative Implies Lipschitz theorem (Lecture 18), f(x)=11+x2f(x) = \dfrac{1}{1+x^2}f(x)=1+x21​ is Lipschitz on R\mathbb{R}R with constant k=1k=1k=1, and therefore uniformly continuous on all of R\mathbb{R}R — with no need to split the domain, unlike the x\sqrt{x}x​ example above, precisely because the derivative here is bounded globally rather than just locally.
